Problem Statement:
Let (\mathbb{Q}) denote the field of rational numbers, and consider the polynomial:
[
f(x) = x^5 - 4x + 2 \in \mathbb{Q}[x].
]

Let (K) denote the splitting field of the polynomial (f(x)) over (\mathbb{Q}), and let (G = \text{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})) denote the Galois group of the polynomial.

Prove the following statements clearly and rigorously:

(a) Prove that the polynomial ( f(x) ) is irreducible over (\mathbb{Q}).

(b) Show that the Galois group (G=\text{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})) is isomorphic to a transitive subgroup of the symmetric group (S_5). Argue that (G) contains a transposition (a 2-cycle).

(c) Using results from algebra and number theory, determine the parity of the discriminant of the polynomial (f(x)). Furthermore, conclude whether or not (G) is isomorphic to the full symmetric group (S_5).

(d) Given your conclusions above, determine explicitly the number of subfields of the splitting field (K) that are quadratic extensions of (\mathbb{Q}).

Here is a complete solution, broken into the four parts you asked for. 

**(a) Irreducibility of f(x)=x⁵–4x+2 over ℚ**  
We apply Eisenstein’s criterion at p=2.  Write  
 f(x)=x⁵+0·x⁴+0·x³+0·x²+(–4)x+2.  
All non‐leading coefficients are divisible by 2, and the constant term 2 is _not_ divisible by 2²=4.  Hence by Eisenstein at p=2, f is irreducible in ℚ[x].  

**(b) G=Gal(K/ℚ) is a transitive subgroup of S₅, and contains a transposition**  
1.  Since f is irreducible of degree 5, its Galois group G acts transitively on the five roots.  Hence we may view  
 G⊂S₅  
as a transitive subgroup.  

2.  To see that G contains a 2‐cycle (a transposition), one uses the **Chebotarev density theorem** (or simply tests mod p factorisations for small p).  Concretely, one checks (e.g. by hand or a computer) that for p=41  
 f(x) mod 41 factors in 𝔽₄₁[x] as  
  a single irreducible quadratic · three distinct linear factors.  
By the Frobenius‐cycle‐type dictionary this says that (in cycle‐notation) some element of G acts on the five roots as a 2‐cycle and three 1‐cycles—that is, as a transposition in S₅.  

**(c) Discriminant and “full” Galois group**  
1.  For a quintic of the form x⁵+ax+b one has the well‐known closed‐form  
 Disc(x⁵+ax+b) = –256 a⁵ – 3125 b⁴.  
Here a=–4, b=2, so  
 Disc(f)=–256·(–4)⁵ – 3125·2⁴  
  = –256·(–1024) – 3125·16  
  = +256 × 1024 – 50000  
  = 262144 – 50000  
  = 212144  
  = 16·13259.  
Since 13259 is square‐free, Disc(f) is _not_ a square in ℚ.  

2.  On the other hand, any transitive subgroup of A₅ (the even permutations) would force the discriminant to be a square in ℚ.  Because we have shown Disc(f)∉(ℚ^×)², it follows that  
 G≇A₅  
and so the only possibility for a transitive subgroup of S₅ which contains an odd permutation and a transposition is the _whole_ S₅.  Hence  
 G≅S₅.  

**(d) Quadratic subfields of the splitting field K**  
Galois‐correspondence says that quadratic subfields of K (i.e. extensions of degree 2 over ℚ) correspond exactly to index‐2 subgroups of G.  In S₅ there is a unique subgroup of index 2, namely A₅.  Therefore there is exactly **one** quadratic subfield of K.  Concretely it is  
 ℚ(√Disc(f)) = ℚ(√13259).  

—

**Summary of answers**  
(a) By Eisenstein at 2, f is irreducible in ℚ[x].  
(b) Irreducibility ⇒ G is transitive on 5 roots; a mod‑41 (for instance) factorization 2+1+1+1 forces G to contain a transposition.  
(c) Disc(f)=16·13259 is _not_ a square ⇒ G contains odd permutations but isn’t contained in A₅ ⇒ G≅S₅.  
(d) There is exactly one quadratic subfield (fixed by A₅), namely ℚ(√13259).

Problem Statement:
Let (\mathbb{Q}) denote the field of rational numbers, and consider the polynomial:
[
f(x) = x^5 - 4x + 2 \in \mathbb{Q}[x].
]

Let (K) denote the splitting field of the polynomial (f(x)) over (\mathbb{Q}), and let (G = \text{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})) denote the Galois group of the polynomial.

Prove the following statements clearly and rigorously:

(a) Prove that the polynomial ( f(x) ) is irreducible over (\mathbb{Q}).

(b) Show that the Galois group (G=\text{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})) is isomorphic to a transitive subgroup of the symmetric group (S_5). Argue that (G) contains a transposition (a 2-cycle).

(c) Using results from algebra and number theory, determine the parity of the discriminant of the polynomial (f(x)). Furthermore, conclude whether or not (G) is isomorphic to the full symmetric group (S_5).

(d) Given your conclusions above, determine explicitly the number of subfields of the splitting field (K) that are quadratic extensions of (\mathbb{Q}).

Below is one complete route through the problem.  
(Whenever we speak of “the discriminant’’ we mean the integral discriminant, i.e. the
one obtained from the usual resultant formula; it is an integer because the
coefficients are integers.)

------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  Irreducibility of f(x)=x⁵−4x+2 over ℚ
------------------------------------------------------------
A neat way to see irreducibility is to shift the variable so that Eisenstein’s
criterion with the prime 2 becomes available.

Put x = y+2 and write  

 g(y)=f(y+2)=(y+2)⁵-4(y+2)+2.  

A short binomial expansion gives  

 g(y)=y⁵+10y⁴+40y³+80y²+76y+26.

All coefficients except the leading one are divisible by 2, and the constant
term 26 is **not** divisible by 4.  Hence Eisenstein’s criterion with p=2
applies to g(y), so g(y) is irreducible in ℚ[y].  Because
f(x)=g(x-2), the original polynomial f(x) is irreducible in ℚ[x].

------------------------------------------------------------
(b)  The Galois group embeds in S₅ and contains a transposition
------------------------------------------------------------
• Because f is irreducible of degree 5, adjoining one root gives an
extension of degree 5; therefore Gal(K/ℚ) acts transitively on the five
roots.  This furnishes an injective homomorphism  

 Gal(K/ℚ) ↪ S₅,  

and the image is a transitive subgroup.

• How do we see a transposition?  
  The polynomial has real coefficients.  A quick
sign–change count shows that it possesses exactly three real roots:

 f(−2)=−22<0, f(−1)=5>0  → one real root in (−2,−1),  
 f(0)=2>0,  f(1)=−1<0 → one in (0,1),  
 f(1)=−1<0, f(2)=26>0  → one in (1,2).

Since the degree is 5, the remaining two roots are
complex conjugates.  Complex conjugation fixes the three real roots and
interchanges the two non‑real ones, i.e. it acts as a 2‑cycle
(transposition) on the set of all five roots.  Therefore the
embedded group Gal(K/ℚ) ⊂ S₅ contains an odd permutation,
namely a transposition.

------------------------------------------------------------
(c)  The discriminant and the size of the Galois group
------------------------------------------------------------
Step 1 – Parity (divisibility by 2).  
Reduce f modulo 2:

 f̅(x)=x⁵  in 𝔽₂[x].

In 𝔽₂ the derivative is f̅′(x)=x⁴, so f̅ and f̅′
have the common root x=0; hence the discriminant of f(x) is divisible by 2.
Thus Disc(f) is even.

Step 2 – Whether Disc(f) is a square.  
For any separable polynomial of degree n, Gal(K/ℚ) is contained in Aₙ
iff its discriminant is a square in ℚ.
We already exhibited a transposition in Gal(K/ℚ), so the group
is **not** contained in A₅.  Consequently Disc(f) is **not** a square in ℚ
(indeed it must be 2 · odd).

Step 3 – Concluding that Gal(K/ℚ)=S₅.  
By Cauchy’s theorem the transitive group Gal(K/ℚ) of order divisible by 5
contains an element of order 5; viewed in S₅ such an element is
necessarily a 5‑cycle.  A transposition and a 5‑cycle generate the whole
of S₅, so

 Gal(K/ℚ) ≅ S₅.

(Equivalently: the only transitive subgroups of S₅
that contain a transposition and have order divisible by 5 are S₅ itself.)

------------------------------------------------------------
(d)  Quadratic subfields of the splitting field K
------------------------------------------------------------
By the Galois correspondence, quadratic subfields of K/ℚ are in
one‑to‑one correspondence with index‑2 subgroups of Gal(K/ℚ).  Because

 Gal(K/ℚ)=S₅,

and S₅ has exactly one subgroup of index 2—namely A₅—there is
exactly one quadratic subfield.

Concretely it is the field obtained by adjoining √Disc(f):

 ℚ(√Disc(f))  ⊂ K,

since A₅ is precisely the kernel of the sign homomorphism
S₅ → {±1}, and that kernel fixes √Disc(f).

Hence:

Number of quadratic extensions of ℚ contained in K = 1.

------------------------------------------------------------
Summary
------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  f(x) is irreducible over ℚ (Eisenstein after the shift x→x+2).  
(b)  Gal(K/ℚ) is a transitive subgroup of S₅ and contains a transposition.  
(c)  Disc(f) is even and not a square; therefore Gal(K/ℚ)=S₅.  
(d)  Since S₅ has a unique index‑2 subgroup (A₅), the splitting field K
contains exactly one quadratic subfield, namely ℚ(√Disc(f)).

Problem Statement:
Let (\mathbb{Q}) denote the field of rational numbers, and consider the polynomial:
[
f(x) = x^5 - 4x + 2 \in \mathbb{Q}[x].
]

Let (K) denote the splitting field of the polynomial (f(x)) over (\mathbb{Q}), and let (G = \text{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})) denote the Galois group of the polynomial.

Prove the following statements clearly and rigorously:

(a) Prove that the polynomial ( f(x) ) is irreducible over (\mathbb{Q}).

(b) Show that the Galois group (G=\text{Gal}(K/\mathbb{Q})) is isomorphic to a transitive subgroup of the symmetric group (S_5). Argue that (G) contains a transposition (a 2-cycle).

(c) Using results from algebra and number theory, determine the parity of the discriminant of the polynomial (f(x)). Furthermore, conclude whether or not (G) is isomorphic to the full symmetric group (S_5).

(d) Given your conclusions above, determine explicitly the number of subfields of the splitting field (K) that are quadratic extensions of (\mathbb{Q}).

Here is a complete solution, broken into the four parts you asked for. 

**(a) Irreducibility of f(x)=x⁵–4x+2 over ℚ**  
We apply Eisenstein’s criterion at p=2.  Write  
 f(x)=x⁵+0·x⁴+0·x³+0·x²+(–4)x+2.  
All non‐leading coefficients are divisible by 2, and the constant term 2 is _not_ divisible by 2²=4.  Hence by Eisenstein at p=2, f is irreducible in ℚ[x].  

**(b) G=Gal(K/ℚ) is a transitive subgroup of S₅, and contains a transposition**  
1.  Since f is irreducible of degree 5, its Galois group G acts transitively on the five roots.  Hence we may view  
 G⊂S₅  
as a transitive subgroup.  

2.  To see that G contains a 2‐cycle (a transposition), one uses the **Chebotarev density theorem** (or simply tests mod p factorisations for small p).  Concretely, one checks (e.g. by hand or a computer) that for p=41  
 f(x) mod 41 factors in 𝔽₄₁[x] as  
  a single irreducible quadratic · three distinct linear factors.  
By the Frobenius‐cycle‐type dictionary this says that (in cycle‐notation) some element of G acts on the five roots as a 2‐cycle and three 1‐cycles—that is, as a transposition in S₅.  

**(c) Discriminant and “full” Galois group**  
1.  For a quintic of the form x⁵+ax+b one has the well‐known closed‐form  
 Disc(x⁵+ax+b) = –256 a⁵ – 3125 b⁴.  
Here a=–4, b=2, so  
 Disc(f)=–256·(–4)⁵ – 3125·2⁴  
  = –256·(–1024) – 3125·16  
  = +256 × 1024 – 50000  
  = 262144 – 50000  
  = 212144  
  = 16·13259.  
Since 13259 is square‐free, Disc(f) is _not_ a square in ℚ.  

2.  On the other hand, any transitive subgroup of A₅ (the even permutations) would force the discriminant to be a square in ℚ.  Because we have shown Disc(f)∉(ℚ^×)², it follows that  
 G≇A₅  
and so the only possibility for a transitive subgroup of S₅ which contains an odd permutation and a transposition is the _whole_ S₅.  Hence  
 G≅S₅.  

**(d) Quadratic subfields of the splitting field K**  
Galois‐correspondence says that quadratic subfields of K (i.e. extensions of degree 2 over ℚ) correspond exactly to index‐2 subgroups of G.  In S₅ there is a unique subgroup of index 2, namely A₅.  Therefore there is exactly **one** quadratic subfield of K.  Concretely it is  
 ℚ(√Disc(f)) = ℚ(√13259).  

—

**Summary of answers**  
(a) By Eisenstein at 2, f is irreducible in ℚ[x].  
(b) Irreducibility ⇒ G is transitive on 5 roots; a mod‑41 (for instance) factorization 2+1+1+1 forces G to contain a transposition.  
(c) Disc(f)=16·13259 is _not_ a square ⇒ G contains odd permutations but isn’t contained in A₅ ⇒ G≅S₅.  
(d) There is exactly one quadratic subfield (fixed by A₅), namely ℚ(√13259).

Below is one complete route through the problem.  
(Whenever we speak of “the discriminant’’ we mean the integral discriminant, i.e. the
one obtained from the usual resultant formula; it is an integer because the
coefficients are integers.)

------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  Irreducibility of f(x)=x⁵−4x+2 over ℚ
------------------------------------------------------------

criterion with the prime 2 becomes available.

Put x = y+2 and write

All coefficients except the leading one are divisible by 2, and the constant

------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------
(a)  f(x) is irreducible over ℚ (Eisenstein after the shift x→x+2).

estimate how many games (whatever the time control) top chess players played in their life when they are 30.

Don't forget that online games are a thing.

Here’s a back-of-the-envelope estimate of how many games a “top‐level” player might have under their belt by age 30—counting all OTB (over-the-board) and online blitz/rapid/bullet.  You’ll see there’s a pretty wide band (10 000–60 000+) depending on how much they grind online.

1. Key Assumptions  
   • They start serious tournament play around age 10.  
   • OTB tournament volume grows from ~50 games/year in their teens to ~100 – 150 games/year in their 20s (classical + rapid + blitz).  
   • Online play really ramps up from ~age 15 onward.  A busy GM might do 500–1 500 online games/year across bullet/rapid/blitz.

2. Sample Breakdown  

| Category                    | Games/yr (conservative) | Games/yr (active) | Years active | Total (consv.) | Total (active) |
|-----------------------------|-------------------------:|------------------:|-------------:|---------------:|---------------:|
| OTB classical/rapid/blitz   |                       80 |               150 |           20 |           1 600 |           3 000 |
| Online blitz/rapid/bullet   |                      300 |             1 000 |           15 |           4 500 |          15 000 |
| **Grand total by age 30**   |                          |                   |              |        ≈ 6 100 |       ≈ 18 000 |

3. Wider Range  
  – A **more casual** top‐100 player (fewer online blitz sessions) might end up closer to **5 000–10 000**.  
  – A **full-time GM** who lives on Chess.com/Lichess could easily rack up **20 000–60 000+** games by 30.  
  – World-class super-users (e.g. Carlsen) sometimes top **50 000–100 000** across all platforms.

4. Bottom Line  
  – **Conservative** estimate: ~6 000 games by 30  
  – **Typical busy GM**: ~15 000–25 000  
  – **Ultra-active online star**: ~40 000–60 000 (or more)

Of course, everyone’s regimen is different—some focus almost exclusively on long games with very little online blitz, others do 3 – 4 nightly bullet marathons.  But ballpark, tens of thousands of games is what it takes to reach (and sustain) that 2700+ level.

estimate how many games (whatever the time control) top chess players played in their life when they are 30.

Don't forget that online games are a thing.

It turns out that “how many games” a top player has played by age 30 is a very rough number to pin down—it depends on what you count (and when), and the answer can vary by a full order‐of‐magnitude. Still, here’s one way to break it down:

---

### Tournament & “Over-the‐Board” Games

- **Classical (and official rapid/blitz) tournament games:**  
  Many top players begin playing serious tournaments in their teens. Over a pro’s career one can easily record on the order of a couple of thousand “official” games (classical or even included rapid and blitz events). For example, looking at public databases, you might see that a player like Magnus Carlsen or Viswanathan Anand has a comparable record of, say, 1,500–3,000 well‐documented classical tournament games by their late twenties. (Note that “official” rapid and blitz tournaments get added on top of that in today’s chess world.)  

- **Other “serious” games:**  
  Training sessions, exhibition matches, and classical simul events add some more—but these are often not part of the official record.

So if you just count traditional over‐the‐board and FIDE‐rated events, a top player might have something like 2,000–5,000 games by the time they are 30.

---

### Online Games

The past decade has seen an explosion in online chess. Top professionals often use online play for training, entertainment, or participating in online tournaments. Some points to consider:

- **Frequency:**  
  It’s not unusual for a top player to play anywhere from 20 to 100+ games in a single day if they’re in “online mode” (especially in bullet or blitz formats).

- **Accumulation over years:**  
  Even if a top player played “only” 50 online games a day on average (and that’s a conservative number on days devoted to online blitz, rapid, and bullet), over a year you’re looking at roughly 18,000 games. Over, say, 10–15 years of having online access, that easily sums to tens of thousands—perhaps even over 100,000 games.

Of course, some online games may be played more casually or in training, and not every single one has the same “weight” in a player’s mental currency, but they still count as chess games.

---

### Putting It Together

- **Official/Uptown Tournament Games:** Approximately **2,000–5,000 games** by age 30.  
- **Online & Informal Games:** Easily **tens of thousands**—and if you really add up all the quick bullet, blitz, and rapid games played online, some estimates could run from **20,000 up to well over 100,000 games** by the time a top player turns 30.

So, a rough “back-of-the‐envelope” answer is that:

- **If you count only formal, over‐the‐board tournament games:** a top player might have around **a few thousand games.**
- **If you include every kind of rated (or even untitled) online game, drill, or blitz session:** the total number becomes much larger—likely in the range of **tens of thousands to over 100,000 games.**

This wide range reflects both the evolution of the sport (with online chess in the mix) and differences in training routines and tournament schedules. Each player’s “game count” can be very different.

solve x+1=x+1

To solve the equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \), follow these steps:

1. **Subtract \( x \) from both sides:**

   \[
   \begin{aligned}
   x + 1 - x &= x + 1 - x \\
   1 &= 1
   \end{aligned}
   \]

2. **Analyze the Result:**

   The resulting equation \( 1 = 1 \) is always true, regardless of the value of \( x \). This means that the original equation is an identity.

3. **Conclusion:**

   Since the equation holds true for all values of \( x \), the solution is:

   \[
   x \in \mathbb{R}
   \]

In other words, **any real number** is a solution to the equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \).

**Example: Solving the Equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \)**

Consider the equation:

\[
x + 1 = x + 1
\]

**Step 1. Eliminate the Variable \( x \):**

Subtract \( x \) from both sides of the equation:

\[
\begin{aligned}
x + 1 - x &= x + 1 - x \\
1 &= 1
\end{aligned}
\]

**Step 2. Analyze the Result:**

In arriving at the statement \( 1 = 1 \), we see that this is an identity—it is true for every possible value of \( x \). Since subtracting \( x \) did not alter the equality, the original equation does not impose any restriction on \( x \).

**Conclusion:**

The equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \) is an identity, which means that it is true for all values of \( x \). Thus, the solution to the equation is:

\[
x \in \mathbb{R}
\]

In summary, any real number is a solution to the equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \).
solve x+1=x+1

The equation  
```  
x + 1 = x + 1  
```  
is an identity. Here’s why:

1. Subtract x from both sides:  
   (x + 1) – x = (x + 1) – x  
2. This simplifies to:  
   1 = 1  

Since 1 = 1 is always true, **every real number** (indeed every complex number) x satisfies the equation.  

**Solution set:**  
All real numbers, i.e. ℝ (or more generally, all x∈ℂ).

## Example 1.1 (Identity Equation)

**Problem.** Solve the equation
\[
x + 1 \;=\; x + 1.
\]

---

### Solution

We recognize that this is an **identity**, i.e. an equation that holds for all permissible values of the unknown. To see this algebraically, we proceed by the Subtraction Property of Equality:

1. **Subtract** \(x\) from both sides:  
   \[
   \begin{aligned}
   (x + 1) - x &= (x + 1) - x,\\
   1 &= 1.
   \end{aligned}
   \]

2. Since the resulting statement \(1 = 1\) is **always true**, the original equation imposes **no restriction** on \(x\).

---

### Conclusion

- The given equation is an **identity**.
- **Solution set:** All real numbers  
  \[
    \{\,x \in \mathbb{R} : x + 1 = x + 1\} \;=\; \mathbb{R}.
  \]

> **Remark.**  
> An identity of the form  
> \(\;x + a = x + a\)\  
> is always true, for any constant \(a\).

\begin{aligned}
x + 1 - x &= x + 1 - x \\
1 &= 1
\end{aligned}
\]

**Step 2. Analyze the Result:**

In arriving at the statement \( 1 = 1 \), we see that this is an identity—it is true for every possible value of \( x \). Since subtracting \( x \) did not alter the equality, the original equation does not impose any restriction on \( x \).

**Conclusion:**

The equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \) is an identity, which means that it is true for all values of \( x \). Thus, the solution to the equation is:

\[
x \in \mathbb{R}
\]

In summary, any real number is a solution to the equation \( x + 1 = x + 1 \).

## Example 1.1 (Identity Equation)

**Problem.** Solve the equation
\[
x + 1 \;=\; x + 1.
\]

---

### Solution

We recognize that this is an **identity**, i.e. an equation that holds for all permissible values of the unknown. To see this algebraically, we proceed by the Subtraction Property of Equality:

1. **Subtract** \(x\) from both sides:  
   \[
   \begin{aligned}
   (x + 1) - x &= (x + 1) - x,\\
   1 &= 1.
   \end{aligned}
   \]

2. Since the resulting statement \(1 = 1\) is **always true**, the original equation imposes **no restriction** on \(x\).

---

### Conclusion

- The given equation is an **identity**.
- **Solution set:** All real numbers  
  \[
    \{\,x \in \mathbb{R} : x + 1 = x + 1\} \;=\; \mathbb{R}.
  \]

> **Remark.**  
> An identity of the form  
> \(\;x + a = x + a\)\  
> is always true, for any constant \(a\).

My model learns user embeddings for recommendations. Model uses average of user embedding and hist historical item embeddings. My hypothesis is that user embeddings are nothing but noise. How can I prove it?

Below is a “tool-box” of practical checks you can run.  
If several of them converge to “the user-id vector carries no signal”, you’ll have convincing evidence that the embedding is just noise.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Straight-up ablation (a.k.a. remove it and see what happens)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Model A  = ⟨user emb + mean(history)⟩ → predict  
Model B  = ⟨mean(history) only⟩ → predict  

• Train both with identical data, optimizer, seeds, #epochs.  
• Evaluate on an unseen test set with the metric that matters to you (AUC, NDCG@k, MAP, HR, etc.).  
• Do a paired bootstrap significance test (or paired t-test) on the user-level scores.

If Model B is not statistically worse (Δmetric≈0, p>0.05), the user vector is dispensable.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2. Permutation / shuffling test
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Keep the full network but, at inference time, randomly permute user embeddings between users:

û_i  = user_emb(π(i))        // π = random permutation  

If performance stays unchanged, the mapping “user-id → embedding” is irrelevant → noise.

Variant: Replace each user embedding with i.i.d. Gaussian noise of same variance as the learnt embeddings; measure again.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3. Freeze-at-random initialization
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Initialize user embeddings, then freeze them (no gradient updates).  
If the final test metric is indistinguishable from the fully-trained run, the learned values were pointless.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4. Learnable mixing weight (gating)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Make the final representation a convex combination and LET THE MODEL decide:

h = α · user_emb + (1-α) · mean(history)  
α = sigmoid(w)                       // single scalar parameter

After training, check the learnt α:  
• α → 0  ⇒ model suppresses user emb.  
• α → 1  ⇒ history ignored (probably not your case).  

Confidence intervals for α around 0 mean “user vector not useful”.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5. Information–content probes
--------------------------------------------------------------------
a) Visualization: t-SNE / UMAP of the user embeddings, color points by  
   – #purchases, dominant category, geography, age bracket, churn label…  
   No visible structure → low information content.

b) Down-stream prediction: train a simple logistic regression / MLP on frozen user embeddings to predict any auxiliary user attribute you have.  
   Chance-level accuracy ≈ noise.

c) Nearest-neighbor coherence: for each user, take k nearest neighbors in embedding space; measure Jaccard overlap of their historical item sets. If overlap is no higher than for random pairs, embeddings are not capturing preference similarity.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6. Regularisation stress test
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Add very strong L2 or L1 penalty ONLY on the user vectors.  
If they all collapse to ~0 without hurting the metric, they were not carrying signal.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7. Training-dynamics look
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Track the average norm of user embeddings during training.  
• Flat or erratic evolution compared to item-emb norms often means gradients through that branch are negligible.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
8. Log-likelihood attribution
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For probabilistic recommenders (e.g., sampled softmax):  
Compute the per-example log-prob with  
  (i) full input,  
  (ii) zeroed user-emb,  
  (iii) zeroed history term.  
ΔLL_user ≈ 0 ⇒ user term adds no explanatory power.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Putting it all together
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Recommended minimal proof-of-noise checklist:

✓ Run ablation (Step 1) → insignificant Δmetric  
✓ Run permutation (Step 2) → insignificant Δmetric  
✓ Check learnt α (Step 4) → α ≈ 0  

Three independent signals pointing the same way are usually enough to convince reviewers / your boss / yourself.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
What if the embeddings ARE noise? A few next steps
--------------------------------------------------------------------
1. Delete them: simpler model, smaller memory footprint.  
2. Replace with explicit user features (demographics, marketing segments).  
3. Introduce a time-aware history encoder (RNN/Transformer) so the model relies on behaviour, not id.  
4. Use two-tower (user tower gets behavioural features, no frozen id).

Good luck, and happy disproving!

My model learns user embeddings for recommendations. Model uses average of user embedding and hist historical item embeddings. My hypothesis is that user embeddings are nothing but noise. How can I prove it?

Great question! If you suspect that your **user embeddings** are "nothing but noise" (i.e., not learning meaningful information), you can **test this hypothesis** in several ways. Here are some practical approaches:

---

### 1. **Ablation Study: Remove User Embeddings**

- **What to do:**  
  Train and evaluate your model **without** the user embedding (i.e., use only the average of historical item embeddings).
- **How to interpret:**  
  - If performance (e.g., accuracy, NDCG, AUC) **does not drop** (or even improves), it suggests user embeddings are not contributing useful information.
  - If performance **drops significantly**, user embeddings are likely meaningful.

---

### 2. **Randomization Test**

- **What to do:**  
  Replace user embeddings with **random vectors** (sampled from the same distribution as your learned embeddings).
- **How to interpret:**  
  - If model performance is **similar** to using learned user embeddings, it suggests the learned embeddings are not encoding useful information (i.e., they're like noise).
  - If performance **drops**, the learned embeddings are likely meaningful.

---

### 3. **Visualization & Clustering**

- **What to do:**  
  Use dimensionality reduction (e.g., t-SNE, PCA) to visualize user embeddings.  
  Try clustering (e.g., K-means) and see if clusters correspond to meaningful user groups (e.g., by demographics or behavior).
- **How to interpret:**  
  - If embeddings are **randomly scattered** with no structure, they may be noise.
  - If you see **clusters** that correspond to real user segments, embeddings are meaningful.

---

### 4. **Correlation with User Features**

- **What to do:**  
  If you have user metadata (age, location, etc.), check if user embeddings correlate with these features.
- **How to interpret:**  
  - **No correlation** suggests embeddings are not capturing user characteristics (possibly noise).
  - **Significant correlation** suggests embeddings are meaningful.

---

### 5. **Embedding Norms and Distribution**

- **What to do:**  
  Analyze the distribution of user embedding norms (L2 norm, variance, etc.).
- **How to interpret:**  
  - If all embeddings are close to zero or have very similar values, they may not be learning anything useful.

---

## **Summary Table**

| Test                | What to do                        | What it shows if user embeddings are noise      |
|---------------------|-----------------------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| Ablation            | Remove user embeddings            | No performance drop                            |
| Randomization       | Replace with random vectors       | No performance drop                            |
| Visualization       | t-SNE/PCA, clustering             | No structure, random scatter                   |
| Correlation         | Compare with user features        | No correlation                                 |
| Norms/Distribution  | Analyze embedding values          | All similar, low variance                      |

---

## **Recommended Steps**

1. **Start with the ablation and randomization tests**—these are the most direct.
2. **Visualize and analyze** if you want more insight into what the embeddings are (or aren't) capturing.

---

**If you want code examples or more details on any of these steps, let me know!**

An interesting metric I can think of that can measure human technological progress is the maximum speed a human has ever achieved. You are asked to gather information and eventually make a chart with time on the horizontal axis and speed on the vertical axis. You have to spend two lines, one for the maximum speed ever reached among all humans, and the other for the maximum speed ever reached by the 50th percentile of humans. You're also going to inscribe some specific points of time and speed. Remember to generate a graph directly.

Below is a ready-to-use line chart that visualises “how fast a human can go” through history.  
(The graphic is rendered on-the-fly by QuickChart, so it should show up automatically in most browsers.)

![Human Speed Records Over Time](https://quickchart.io/chart?c=%7B%22type%22%3A%22line%22%2C%22data%22%3A%7B%22labels%22%3A%5B%221800%22%2C%221880%22%2C%221903%22%2C%221947%22%2C%221961%22%2C%221969%22%2C%222020%22%5D%2C%22datasets%22%3A%5B%7B%22label%22%3A%22Fastest%20Human%20Alive%20(absolute%20record)%22%2C%22borderColor%22%3A%22%23e74c3c%22%2C%22pointStyle%22%3A%22rectRot%22%2C%22data%22%3A%5B25%2C50%2C56%2C434%2C2400%2C11083%2C11083%5D%7D%2C%7B%22label%22%3A%22Fastest%20Speed%20Reached%20by%20the%2050th%20Percentile%20Human%22%2C%22borderColor%22%3A%22%233498db%22%2C%22pointStyle%22%3A%22circle%22%2C%22data%22%3A%5B3%2C13%2C25%2C30%2C240%2C240%2C250%5D%7D%5D%7D%2C%22options%22%3A%7B%22title%22%3A%7B%22display%22%3Atrue%2C%22text%22%3A%22Human%20Speed%20Records%20Through%20Time%20(m%2Fs)%22%7D%2C%22legend%22%3A%7B%22position%22%3A%22bottom%22%7D%2C%22scales%22%3A%7B%22yAxes%22%3A%5B%7B%22type%22%3A%22linear%22%2C%22scaleLabel%22%3A%7B%22display%22%3Atrue%2C%22labelString%22%3A%22Speed%20(metres%20per%20second)%22%7D%2C%22ticks%22%3A%7B%22beginAtZero%22%3Atrue%7D%7D%5D%2C%22xAxes%22%3A%5B%7B%22scaleLabel%22%3A%7B%22display%22%3Atrue%2C%22labelString%22%3A%22Year%22%7D%7D%5D%7D%7D%7D)

What the two lines represent  
• Red — “Fastest Human”: the single greatest speed any human being has ever experienced up to that year.  
• Blue — “Median Human (50 th %ile)”: the fastest speed a typical person could realistically hit (e.g., by hopping on whatever technology was widely available).

Annotated milestones (all speeds converted to m/s):

Fastest-Human curve  
•  ~1800 — 25 m/s (90 km/h)  | Expert horse rider at full gallop  
•  1880 — 50 m/s (180 km/h)  | Experimental steam locomotive “City of Truro” era records  
•  1903 — 56 m/s (200 km/h)  | Early land-speed-record automobiles  
•  1947 — 434 m/s (1 570 km/h) | Chuck Yeager, Bell X-1, first to break Mach 1  
•  1961 — 2 400 m/s (8 640 km/h) | Yuri Gagarin’s Vostok 1 re-entry  
•  1969 — 11 083 m/s (39 897 km/h) | Apollo 10 return from lunar orbit (still the all-time human speed record)  
•  2020 — 11 083 m/s (record unchanged since 1969)

50 th-Percentile curve (fastest trip an “average” person could plausibly take)
•  1800 — 3 m/s  (jogging)  
•  1880 — 13 m/s  (passenger steam trains ≈ 50 km/h)  
•  1903 — 25 m/s  (early mass-market motorcars)  
•  1947 — 30 m/s  (typical rail/highway travel)  
•  1961 — 240 m/s  (first generation jet-liners such as the Boeing 707)  
•  1969 — 240 m/s  (commercial jets plateau)  
•  2020 — 250 m/s  (modern long-haul airliners)

Key take-aways  
• Technological breakthroughs for the extreme edge (rocketry) saw 3–4 orders-of-magnitude growth in < 200 years.  
• The “median human” curve lags badly once commercial jet speeds plateaued; supersonic travel (Concorde, upcoming Boom Overture) could nudge it upward, but nowhere near Apollo-level velocities.  
• Unless point-to-point sub-orbital flights, Hyperloop-scale maglev, or widespread supersonic service become mainstream, the gap between the fastest human ever and the typical human will continue to widen.

An interesting metric I can think of that can measure human technological progress is the maximum speed a human has ever achieved. You are asked to gather information and eventually make a chart with time on the horizontal axis and speed on the vertical axis. You have to spend two lines, one for the maximum speed ever reached among all humans, and the other for the maximum speed ever reached by the 50th percentile of humans. You're also going to inscribe some specific points of time and speed. Remember to generate a graph directly.

That's a fascinating way to visualize human technological progress! I'll gather some key data points for maximum human speed over time, both for the absolute maximum speed ever reached by any human (including vehicles, spacecraft, etc.) and for the 50th percentile human speed (roughly the average human running or moving speed).

### Key points to consider:

- **Maximum speed ever reached by any human (absolute max):**
  - Early humans: walking/running speeds (~5-10 km/h)
  - Ancient chariots/horses: up to ~50 km/h
  - Early cars (early 1900s): ~100-150 km/h
  - Modern cars (mid 1900s): ~300 km/h
  - Jet aircraft (1940s-50s): ~2,000 km/h
  - Spacecraft reentry speeds (Apollo missions, 1969): ~39,000 km/h (11 km/s)
  - Spacecraft escape velocity (Voyager 1, 1977): ~61,000 km/h (17 km/s)

- **Maximum speed for the 50th percentile human:**
  - Walking speed: ~5 km/h (average adult walking speed)
  - Running speed: average jogging ~8-10 km/h, average sprint ~15 km/h
  - Cycling speed (average cyclist): ~25-30 km/h
  - Average car speed (commuting): ~50-100 km/h (but this is vehicle speed, not human-powered)
  
Since the 50th percentile human speed is about human-powered or typical human movement speed, it won't increase dramatically with technology unless we consider transportation means used by the average person (e.g., cars, trains). For this graph, I will consider the average human speed as the average speed a typical human can move under normal conditions, including transportation technology accessible to the average person.

---

### Data points for the graph:

| Year | Max Speed (km/h) - Absolute Max | Max Speed (km/h) - 50th Percentile Human |
|-------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| 10,000 BC | 15 (running) | 5 (walking) |
| 2000 BC | 50 (chariot) | 5 (walking) |
| 1900 | 150 (early cars) | 10 (running) |
| 1950 | 2,000 (jet aircraft) | 30 (cycling) |
| 1969 | 39,000 (Apollo reentry) | 100 (car average speed) |
| 1977 | 61,000 (Voyager 1) | 100 (car average speed) |
| 2024 | 61,000+ (spacecraft) | 100 (car average speed) |

---

### Now, I'll generate the graph:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Data
years = np.array([ -8000, 2000, 1900, 1950, 1969, 1977, 2024])
max_speed = np.array([15, 50, 150, 2000, 39000, 61000, 61000])
median_speed = np.array([5, 5, 10, 30, 100, 100, 100])

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.plot(years, max_speed, label='Max Speed Ever Reached by Any Human (km/h)', marker='o')
plt.plot(years, median_speed, label='Max Speed for 50th Percentile Human (km/h)', marker='o')

# Log scale for speed to better visualize wide range
plt.yscale('log')

# Labels and title
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Speed (km/h) [Log Scale]')
plt.title('Human Technological Progress Measured by Maximum Speed Achieved')
plt.grid(True, which="both", ls="--", linewidth=0.5)
plt.legend()

# Annotate key points
annotations = {
    -8000: "Early humans running",
    2000: "Chariots",
    1900: "Early cars",
    1950: "Jet aircraft",
    1969: "Apollo reentry",
    1977: "Voyager 1",
    2024: "Modern spacecraft"
}

for year, text in annotations.items():
    plt.annotate(text, (year, max_speed[np.where(years == year)][0]),
                 textcoords="offset points", xytext=(0,10), ha='center')

plt.show()
```

•  1947 — 434 m/s (1 570 km/h) | Chuck Yeager, Bell X-1, first to break Mach 1  
•  1961 — 2 400 m/s (8 640 km/h) | Yuri Gagarin’s Vostok 1 re-entry  
•  1969 — 11 083 m/s (39 897 km/h) | Apollo 10 return from lunar orbit (still the all-time human speed record)  
•  2020 — 11 083 m/s (record unchanged since 1969)

50 th-Percentile curve (fastest trip an “average” person could plausibly take)
•  1800 — 3 m/s  (jogging)  
•  1880 — 13 m/s  (passenger steam trains ≈ 50 km/h)  
•  1903 — 25 m/s  (early mass-market motorcars)  
•  1947 — 30 m/s  (typical rail/highway travel)  
•  1961 — 240 m/s  (first generation jet-liners such as the Boeing 707)  
•  1969 — 240 m/s  (commercial jets plateau)  
•  2020 — 250 m/s  (modern long-haul airliners)

Key take-aways  
• Technological breakthroughs for the extreme edge (rocketry) saw 3–4 orders-of-magnitude growth in < 200 years.  
• The “median human” curve lags badly once commercial jet speeds plateaued; supersonic travel (Concorde, upcoming Boom Overture) could nudge it upward, but nowhere near Apollo-level velocities.  
• Unless point-to-point sub-orbital flights, Hyperloop-scale maglev, or widespread supersonic service become mainstream, the gap between the fastest human ever and the typical human will continue to widen.

That's a fascinating way to visualize human technological progress! I'll gather some key data points for maximum human speed over time, both for the absolute maximum speed ever reached by any human (including vehicles, spacecraft, etc.) and for the 50th percentile human speed (roughly the average human running or moving speed).

### Key points to consider:

- **Maximum speed ever reached by any human (absolute max):**
  - Early humans: walking/running speeds (~5-10 km/h)
  - Ancient chariots/horses: up to ~50 km/h
  - Early cars (early 1900s): ~100-150 km/h
  - Modern cars (mid 1900s): ~300 km/h
  - Jet aircraft (1940s-50s): ~2,000 km/h
  - Spacecraft reentry speeds (Apollo missions, 1969): ~39,000 km/h (11 km/s)
  - Spacecraft escape velocity (Voyager 1, 1977): ~61,000 km/h (17 km/s)

- **Maximum speed for the 50th percentile human:**
  - Walking speed: ~5 km/h (average adult walking speed)
  - Running speed: average jogging ~8-10 km/h, average sprint ~15 km/h
  - Cycling speed (average cyclist): ~25-30 km/h
  - Average car speed (commuting): ~50-100 km/h (but this is vehicle speed, not human-powered)
  
Since the 50th percentile human speed is about human-powered or typical human movement speed, it won't increase dramatically with technology unless we consider transportation means used by the average person (e.g., cars, trains). For this graph, I will consider the average human speed as the average speed a typical human can move under normal conditions, including transportation technology accessible to the average person.

---

### Data points for the graph:

| Year | Max Speed (km/h) - Absolute Max | Max Speed (km/h) - 50th Percentile Human |
|-------|-------------------------------|-----------------------------------------|
| 10,000 BC | 15 (running) | 5 (walking) |
| 2000 BC | 50 (chariot) | 5 (walking) |
| 1900 | 150 (early cars) | 10 (running) |
| 1950 | 2,000 (jet aircraft) | 30 (cycling) |
| 1969 | 39,000 (Apollo reentry) | 100 (car average speed) |
| 1977 | 61,000 (Voyager 1) | 100 (car average speed) |
| 2024 | 61,000+ (spacecraft) | 100 (car average speed) |

---

### Now, I'll generate the graph:

```python
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Data
years = np.array([ -8000, 2000, 1900, 1950, 1969, 1977, 2024])
max_speed = np.array([15, 50, 150, 2000, 39000, 61000, 61000])
median_speed = np.array([5, 5, 10, 30, 100, 100, 100])

# Plot
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
plt.plot(years, max_speed, label='Max Speed Ever Reached by Any Human (km/h)', marker='o')
plt.plot(years, median_speed, label='Max Speed for 50th Percentile Human (km/h)', marker='o')

# Log scale for speed to better visualize wide range
plt.yscale('log')

# Labels and title
plt.xlabel('Year')
plt.ylabel('Speed (km/h) [Log Scale]')
plt.title('Human Technological Progress Measured by Maximum Speed Achieved')
plt.grid(True, which="both", ls="--", linewidth=0.5)
plt.legend()

# Annotate key points
annotations = {
    -8000: "Early humans running",
    2000: "Chariots",
    1900: "Early cars",
    1950: "Jet aircraft",
    1969: "Apollo reentry",
    1977: "Voyager 1",
    2024: "Modern spacecraft"
}

for year, text in annotations.items():
    plt.annotate(text, (year, max_speed[np.where(years == year)][0]),
                 textcoords="offset points", xytext=(0,10), ha='center')

plt.show()
```
